questionText: A commercial parking garage has 20 L2 EVSEs on a managed circuit. Three units on the east row trip their GFCI every morning around 6 AM but reset fine and run all day. The west row runs without issue. The pattern most likely points to:

choices.text: Defective GFCI modules in all three east units — replace the GFCI boards
Moisture condensation in east-row cable jackets or connectors accumulating overnight and leaking at dawn — megger the cables and inspect connector seals
Load management software incorrectly assigning the east row at 6 AM
The utility voltage sags at 6 AM causing nuisance GFCI trips

explanation: Dawn-only GFCI trips on a geographically grouped set of units point to environmental moisture — condensation in cable jackets, connector seals, or the EVSE housing draining overnight. The east exposure may have higher moisture or lower temperatures. Megger the cables and inspect seals before replacing any electronics.

questionText: A 210 kW DCFC (seven 30 kW modules) delivers a maximum of 150 kW. The status screen shows modules 1–5 healthy and modules 6–7 faulted on 'Internal Comm Error.' The first service action is:

choices.text: Replace all seven modules
Verify modules 6 and 7 firmware matches the controller requirement, then reseat their communication connections before condemning the bricks
Upgrade the AC feed — the charger is under-powered
Increase the output current limit in the network portal

explanation: Internal comm errors between the controller and specific modules often result from firmware version mismatch or a loose communication bus connection before hardware failure. Verify firmware match and reseat connections first — this diagnostic step costs nothing and resolves many brick fault calls.

questionText: A highway corridor DCFC site has four dispensers. All four show 'Unavailable' in the network portal. On-site the screens display normal status and show 'Available.' Last heartbeat was 47 minutes ago. The site has cellular connectivity only. The most efficient diagnostic sequence is:

choices.text: Open each cabinet and inspect the power modules
Check the cellular modem status on each unit, verify SIM seating and signal quality, attempt a RemoteReset via the portal on one unit
Power cycle the entire site at the main disconnect
Replace the cellular modem in all four units

explanation: Four units simultaneously offline with lit screens and no hardware alarms — but no heartbeat — is a site-level connectivity event, not a hardware failure. Cellular modem status and signal quality are the first check; RemoteReset on one unit confirms whether the link recovers remotely before any site visit action.

questionText: A 350 kW DCFC dispenser starts every session at rated power, then derates to 120 kW within 8–10 minutes. No module faults are logged. The connector handle is warm after derating. Pin resistance on the dispenser connector measures 22 mΩ against a 5 mΩ specification. The correct action is:

choices.text: Update the charger firmware to adjust the derating threshold
Replace the connector assembly — pin resistance 4× over spec is causing thermal derating; the connector will continue to degrade
Reduce maximum session power to 120 kW permanently
Replace the liquid-cooled cable

explanation: 22 mΩ is 4× the 5 mΩ specification — at 500 A this dissipates 5.5 W in the connector interface, and the warm handle confirms it is happening. Connector replacement restores rated operation. The cable is not at fault; the connector assembly is the wear item here.

questionText: A fleet depot operator adds four new 150 kW DCFCs to an existing site, all charging simultaneously during an 8 AM shift start. Their monthly electricity bill spikes by $18,000. Their utility charges $15/kW demand. The approximate demand increase the new chargers created was:

choices.text: Approximately 100 kW
Approximately 600 kW — four 150 kW chargers running simultaneously creates a 600 kW demand peak
Approximately 1,200 kW
Approximately 50 kW

explanation: $18,000 / $15 per kW = 1,200 kW total demand billed — but this includes the existing site demand. Four 150 kW chargers adding 600 kW new peak demand accounts for $9,000 of the increase; if existing site load was 600 kW, total new demand is 1,200 kW. The charger contribution alone is 4 × 150 = 600 kW. Load management staggering their start times would reduce this peak.
referenceBookSection: Module 16 — Site Power, Utility & Load Management

questionText: During a preventive maintenance visit to a workplace L2 fleet installation, you notice one connector handle has a faint burning smell and brown discoloration on the J1772 power pins. The correct action is:

choices.text: Clean the pins with electrical contact cleaner and return to service
Flag the unit out of service and replace the connector assembly — discoloration with burning smell indicates resistive heating that will progress to failure
Reduce the EVSE to 16 A operation and monitor for one week
Replace the entire EVSE — connector damage means the unit is unrepairable

explanation: Burning smell plus pin discoloration is the thermal-damage signature of elevated contact resistance. Cleaning is not a remediation — the pin surface is already damaged and resistance will remain elevated. Replace the connector, return to service after verification.

questionText: A fleet manager asks why their new vehicles with NACS inlets cannot use the older CCS-only DCFC stations at their facility. The technically accurate explanation is:

choices.text: NACS and CCS use different voltages
NACS and CCS are physically incompatible connector standards — the vehicle inlet and charger handle must match or a certified adapter is required
NACS vehicles require a higher power level than CCS stations provide
CCS stations are limited to 50 kW and cannot serve NACS vehicles

explanation: NACS (two-pin compact) and CCS (J1772 AC plus two DC pins below) are physically different connector systems. Power levels and voltages can be compatible; the mechanical interface is not. Certified adapters exist, and some newer DCFC units support both standards with selectable cables.

questionText: You are about to replace a faulted power module inside a 350 kW DCFC cabinet. You have opened the AC main disconnect. The manufacturer's service manual specifies a 3-minute discharge wait followed by voltage verification at test point TP-12 to confirm < 50 V DC before touching the internal bus. A colleague says the wait is unnecessary and the bus discharges immediately. The correct action is:

choices.text: Accept the colleague's judgment — experienced techs can skip the wait
Follow the manufacturer procedure: wait the specified 3 minutes, then verify TP-12 < 50 V with a CAT III meter before proceeding
Use a rubber mat instead of waiting — the mat provides sufficient protection
Wait 30 seconds instead of 3 minutes as a compromise

explanation: DC bus capacitors in a 350 kW DCFC hold enough energy to be lethal for seconds to minutes after AC disconnect. The manufacturer's discharge wait time and TP verification are the only acceptable procedure — there is no authority below the manufacturer's service procedure to shorten or skip it. The colleague's opinion does not override the documented safe work procedure.

questionText: A DCFC at a retail site has a completely dark screen and no cooling fan activity. The OCPP portal shows no messages from the unit in 18 hours. The upstream branch breaker is tripped. After resetting the breaker it trips again within 30 seconds. The fault domain is:

choices.text: A network connectivity failure — reseat the cellular modem
A power fault in the charger drawing excess current — the breaker is protecting against a fault in the charger's input stage or internal wiring
A vehicle communication error from the last session
An OCPP firmware error causing hardware to be locked out

explanation: A breaker that resets and re-trips in 30 seconds is responding to a real overcurrent or short-circuit fault in the load — the charger's AC input section. Dark screen with no fan activity confirms no control power, consistent with a hard fault on the AC input. This requires the manufacturer's troubleshooting procedure for input-stage faults before restoring power.

questionText: A user reports they can start sessions with their RFID card but credit card payment sessions fail immediately with 'Transaction Declined' after tapping. OCPP logs show StartTransaction after RFID but no StartTransaction after credit card taps. The fault is:

choices.text: A failed power module — only certain session types can charge
The payment card reader or its backend payment processor integration — RFID authorization succeeds while credit card authorization fails, pointing to the card reader hardware or processor connection
A GFCI fault triggered by credit card tap vibration
An OCPP version mismatch

explanation: RFID sessions succeed through the full OCPP flow while credit card taps fail before StartTransaction — the authorization path is different. Card reader hardware failure or payment processor backend connectivity is the isolated fault domain. Replace or re-pair the card reader, or investigate the payment backend integration.

questionText: A building owner wants to add a 48 A Level 2 EVSE in a parking garage. The existing panel has an open 40 A two-pole breaker slot available. NEC 625.40 compliance requires:

choices.text: The existing 40 A breaker slot is sufficient
A minimum 60 A breaker and conductors rated for 60 A continuous — 48 A × 125% = 60 A, and the 40 A circuit is a code violation for this EVSE
A 50 A breaker is sufficient for a 48 A EVSE
NEC 625 only applies to commercial DCFC, not Level 2

explanation: NEC 625.40: EVSE branch circuits must be sized at 125% of the EVSE continuous current. 48 A × 1.25 = 60 A minimum. The existing 40 A slot cannot serve a 48 A unit — a new 60 A circuit and appropriately rated conductors are required.

questionText: A 150 kW DCFC consistently delivers 90 kW maximum. EMS shows no curtailment. Per-module status: all six 25 kW modules healthy. Connector pin resistance: 3 mΩ (spec ≤ 5 mΩ). Coolant inlet: 44°C (spec < 40°C). The root cause and first corrective action are:

choices.text: A failed power module; replace the brick reading lowest output
Coolant system: elevated inlet temperature is derating the modules — investigate pump flow, heat exchanger condition, coolant level, and loop restrictions
EMS misconfiguration; recheck the portal settings
Connector derating; the 3 mΩ reading is borderline

explanation: EMS clear, all modules healthy, connector excellent — only the coolant inlet at 44°C (4°C above spec) remains. Thermal derating from above-spec coolant temperature is the root cause. Connector resistance is within spec; the cooling loop is the fault domain. Check pump, heat exchanger, coolant level, and inline filter.

questionText: A technician scopes an L2 EVSE pilot circuit with a vehicle connected and finds the signal cycling between State B (±9 V) and State A (+12 V) approximately once per second, even though no session is running. The vehicle is confirmed normal on another EVSE. This behavior indicates:

choices.text: Normal EVSE behavior when in standby mode
The EVSE pilot circuit is intermittently dropping contact with the vehicle — likely a faulty pilot pin or pilot board unable to hold vehicle detection
A utility voltage sag affecting the pilot oscillator
The vehicle is negotiating a higher current level

explanation: State B means the vehicle's resistor is loading the pilot (vehicle connected); State A means the EVSE reads no vehicle. Oscillating between them with a vehicle confirmed connected means the EVSE is intermittently losing contact — faulty pilot pin on the connector or an intermittent pilot board. The pilot board and connector are the investigation.

questionText: At a battery-buffered DCFC site, the BESS BMS is showing a high cell-temperature alarm and has automatically limited its discharge rate to 30%. The DCFCs are derating accordingly. The EMS dashboard shows 'BESS thermal protection active.' This situation requires:

choices.text: Replace the BESS battery modules immediately
Investigate the BESS thermal management system: cooling system status, ambient temperature, and whether the BMS alarm is persistent or transient before any hardware action
Override the BMS thermal limit from the EMS to restore full power
Disconnect the BESS and run chargers from grid only at reduced power

explanation: The BMS is performing its designed protective function. Thermal alarms in a BESS require investigation of the thermal management system (cooling, ambient, load profile) before hardware action — and NEVER overriding the BMS thermal limit, which exists to prevent thermal runaway. The site can continue at reduced power while the root cause is investigated.
referenceBookSection: Module 16 — Site Power, Utility & Load Management

questionText: A customer calls saying their vehicle is stuck to a DCFC dispenser — the session ended but the connector will not release. They are about to try pulling the cable free with their vehicle. The correct advice is:

choices.text: Tell them to pull the vehicle forward slowly — the cable will release
Stop: do not pull with the vehicle. Use the dispenser's emergency connector release procedure (cable pull, UI option, or access panel) to disengage the latch safely
Call the fire department — a stuck connector is a safety emergency
Unplug the dispenser's power to release the latch

explanation: Pulling a vehicle against a latched DCFC connector can damage the vehicle inlet, the connector, and the cable — and in liquid-cooled cables can cause a coolant leak. Every DCFC platform has an emergency release procedure; using it is the correct and documented action. Know it before you get the call.

questionText: A DCFC was working normally yesterday. This morning it boots to a screen displaying 'Firmware Error — Recovery Required.' The network portal shows no heartbeats since 11 PM last night. The operator confirms an OTA firmware update was pushed to the site at 11 PM. The appropriate action is:

choices.text: Replace the control board — firmware errors require hardware replacement
Execute the manufacturer's firmware recovery procedure (USB recovery drive or serial console as specified), then contact support — do not push another OTA update without manufacturer guidance
Attempt another OTA push immediately via the portal
Perform a hard power cycle and wait 24 hours

explanation: Timing correlation with the OTA push makes a failed firmware update the clear diagnosis. Pushing another OTA to a unit in 'Firmware Error' state can corrupt it further. The manufacturer's recovery procedure (USB, serial, or recovery-mode OTA) is the documented path — call support and follow it.

questionText: At a battery-buffered DCFC site, you are about to replace a failed output fuse in the DC distribution section shared by the BESS and the charger. You have performed LOTO on the DCFC's AC main disconnect. What additional step is required before touching the output DC conductors?

choices.text: Nothing additional — LOTO on the charger main is sufficient
Isolate and lock out the BESS DC output as well — two DC sources feed the shared bus; the charger LOTO leaves the BESS energizing the same conductors
Wait for the BESS to discharge fully on its own
Wear rubber gloves — PPE alone is adequate without LOTO on the BESS

explanation: Battery-buffered sites have two DC sources on the output bus: the charger and the BESS. LOTO on the charger's AC main does not isolate the BESS, which may still hold the shared DC bus at full voltage. Two-source LOTO — both charger and BESS isolated — is required before working on any shared DC conductor.

questionText: A network operator's service contract for a new NEVI-funded corridor site specifies 'EVITP-certified technicians for all installation and maintenance.' A technician with this portal's Jr. EV Tech credential but no EVITP certification asks if they qualify. The accurate answer is:

choices.text: Yes — any EV training credential satisfies EVITP requirements
The portal credential demonstrates field service knowledge but does not satisfy an explicit EVITP requirement — EVITP certification must be obtained separately to meet the contract specification
EVITP is only required for utility-scale projects, not corridor DCFC
NEVI-funded projects do not specify technician credentials

explanation: EVITP is a specific credential from the Electric Vehicle Infrastructure Training Program; it is named in contract specifications and NEVI program guidance. Portal credentials and EVITP are complementary — the portal's field service depth plus EVITP installation validation is the competitive combination, but one does not substitute for the other when EVITP is explicitly specified.
referenceBookSection: Module 18 — Career in EV Charging Infrastructure

questionText: A fleet manager complains that vehicles at a 12-port managed L2 installation charge at 16 A in the morning rush but 32 A in the afternoon. They want the system adjusted to always deliver 32 A. The technically correct response is:

choices.text: Upgrade the branch circuit to double the capacity

The load management system is correctly distributing available circuit capacity during peak occupancy — morning 16 A is expected under high occupancy; the circuit cannot support 32 A to all ports simultaneously; add capacity if 32 A per port at peak is required
Disable load management — it is incorrectly configured
Replace the EVSE units with higher-rated models

explanation: Load management throttles per-port current when occupancy is high to stay within the shared circuit rating. Morning rush = many vehicles = lower per-port allocation. Afternoon = fewer vehicles = full 32 A available. The system is working correctly; if 32 A at full occupancy is needed, more electrical capacity is the solution, not a software change.

questionText: A high-traffic DCFC site is experiencing a pattern of scorched EMI filter components on the AC input sections of multiple chargers. Each failure occurs within months of installation. Power modules are healthy. The site shares an industrial transformer with several large HVAC units and a welding shop. The investigation priority is:

choices.text: Replace the AC input boards on all chargers with higher-rated components
Install a power analyzer on the charger AC input for a monitoring period — harmonic distortion and voltage transients from adjacent loads may be stressing the input stage repeatedly
Upgrade the chargers to the next hardware generation
Replace the shared transformer with a dedicated unit for the chargers

explanation: Repeated input-stage failures across multiple units on the same transformer with known harmonic-generating neighbors (HVAC, welding) is the power quality failure signature. A power analyzer confirms the actual harmonic and transient environment before spending money on hardware — the finding then drives the correct solution (dedicated transformer, harmonic filter, surge protection) rather than repeated hardware replacement.
